heroOverview: Water and sanitation-related diseases are caused by pathogens transmitted through contaminated drinking water, inadequate sewage disposal, or poor hygiene — a cluster of exposures the World Health Organization (WHO) calls WASH (water, sanitation, and hygiene) risks. Worldwide, approximately 2 billion people still drink water contaminated with faecal matter, and unsafe water and sanitation together cause an estimated 1.4 million deaths annually, with children under five bearing the greatest burden. Diarrhoeal disease — the most common manifestation — kills around 1.5 million people per year globally, surpassing malaria. Diseases in this group range from acute watery diarrhoea (cholera, enterotoxigenic E. coli) to parasitic infections (giardia, schistosomiasis), viral hepatitis (hepatitis A and E), and environmental toxicant syndromes (fluorosis, arsenicosis). Scaling up access to safe water and improved sanitation produces some of the most cost-effective health gains available in global public health.

keyStats.prevalence: 2 billion people drink water contaminated with faecal matter (WHO, 2023); diarrhoeal diseases cause approximately 1.5 million deaths annually
keyStats.demographics: Children under 5 in low- and middle-income countries (LMICs) bear the highest burden; approximately 450,000 child deaths from diarrhoeal disease annually (WHO, 2022)
keyStats.avgAge: All ages affected; mortality concentrated in children under 5 in LMICs and in immunocompromised adults globally
keyStats.globalCases: An estimated 2.2 billion cases of diarrhoeal disease occur annually; 485,000 cholera cases reported to WHO in 2022 from 44 countries; 2 million new schistosomiasis cases annually

definition: Water and sanitation-related disease (ICD-10: varies by specific condition; grouped under public health categories relating to WASH) encompasses infections and toxic syndromes acquired through three main routes: (1) waterborne transmission — drinking or coming into contact with water containing biological or chemical contaminants; (2) water-washed (water-scarce) transmission — infections that spread because insufficient water is available for basic hygiene such as hand washing, including trachoma and typhoid; and (3) water-based transmission — infections where part of the parasite's lifecycle occurs in water, such as schistosomiasis (cercariae penetrating intact skin in contaminated water). Major pathogen categories include bacteria (Vibrio cholerae, Salmonella typhi, Salmonella paratyphi, enterohaemorrhagic E. coli, Campylobacter, Shigella), viruses (hepatitis A virus, hepatitis E virus, norovirus, rotavirus), protozoa (Giardia lamblia, Cryptosporidium parvum, Entamoeba histolytica), and helminths (Schistosoma species, Dracunculus medinensis, soil-transmitted helminths). Chemical contaminants — particularly arsenic, fluoride, nitrates, and lead — leaching into groundwater produce chronic toxicant syndromes distinct from infectious disease but encompassed in the same WASH framework. The specialty responsible is preventive and public health medicine at the population level; infectious disease physicians, gastroenterologists, paediatricians, and tropical medicine specialists manage individual patients. Safe water is defined by the WHO as water free from microbial, chemical, and radiological hazards and accessible within 30 minutes' round trip, used for drinking, cooking, and personal hygiene.

typesClassification.type: Acute watery diarrhoeal disease (cholera and ETEC)
typesClassification.description: Secretory toxin-mediated diarrhoea characterised by profuse watery stool ('rice-water stools' in cholera) and rapid dehydration; caused by Vibrio cholerae O1/O139 and enterotoxigenic E. coli (ETEC). Case fatality rates reach 25-50% without treatment but fall below 1% with oral rehydration therapy.

Systemic illness from Salmonella typhi or S. paratyphi causing sustained fever, headache, abdominal pain, and rose spots; transmitted via faecally contaminated food and water. Approximately 10-20 million cases annually worldwide.

typesClassification.type: Hepatitis A and E virus infection
typesClassification.description: Faeco-orally transmitted viral hepatitis causing fever, jaundice, and nausea; hepatitis A is vaccine-preventable and resolves in most patients; hepatitis E carries a 10-25% mortality rate in pregnant women.

Cyst-forming protozoa resistant to standard chlorination; produce persistent diarrhoea and malabsorption. Cryptosporidium is the leading cause of waterborne diarrhoeal outbreaks in high-income countries with treated water supplies.

Helminthic infection acquired by wading or swimming in water containing Schistosoma cercariae; affecting approximately 240 million people worldwide. Chronic infection causes haematuria (S. haematobium) or periportal fibrosis and portal hypertension (S. mansoni, S. japonicum).

Chronic exposure to naturally elevated arsenic or fluoride in groundwater causes arsenical keratosis, skin cancer, and peripheral neuropathy (arsenicosis) or dental and skeletal fluorosis, affecting tens of millions of people in South Asia, East Africa, and parts of Latin America.

primarySymptoms: Watery or bloody diarrhoea — the cardinal symptom of most waterborne gastrointestinal infections, ranging from loose stools (giardiasis) to profuse watery diarrhoea with dehydration (cholera) to bloody dysentery (shigellosis, amoebiasis).
Nausea and vomiting — occurring alongside diarrhoea in bacterial and viral gastroenteritis; vomiting further accelerates fluid loss and dehydration.
Fever and chills — systemic inflammatory response indicating bacteraemia or intestinal wall invasion; high sustained fever (39-40°C) for days is characteristic of typhoid fever.
Abdominal cramps and pain — from intestinal inflammation and hypermotility; colicky pain in cholera and campylobacteriosis; constant right iliac fossa pain in typhoid fever.

Signs of dehydration — dry mouth, sunken eyes, reduced skin turgor, decreased urination, and lethargy, which can progress to hypovolaemic shock within hours in severe cholera or rotavirus infection in children.
Jaundice (yellowing of skin and eyes) — a distinctive feature of hepatitis A and E, indicating hepatocellular damage from viral infection contracted through contaminated food or water.
Haematuria (blood in urine) — reddish or brown urine occurring in urogenital schistosomiasis caused by Schistosoma haematobium egg deposition in the bladder wall.
Itching skin rash ('swimmer's itch') — a pruritic papular rash developing within hours of cercarial skin penetration, before systemic schistosomiasis symptoms appear.
Persistent soft stools and bloating with malabsorption — characteristic of giardiasis, with fatty, foul-smelling stools, flatulence, and progressive weight loss over weeks.
Dental mottling and brown staining — a visible sign of dental fluorosis from chronic exposure to fluoride levels above 1.5 mg/L in drinking water, with pitting of enamel in more severe cases.
earlyWarningSigns: Onset of loose or frequent stools within 6-48 hours of consuming water from an unverified source, a shared well, or a flood-affected supply.
Mild nausea, anorexia, and fatigue appearing 15-50 days after travel to an area with inadequate sanitation, suggesting incubating hepatitis A.
Increasing thirst, reduced urine output, and darker urine colour in a young child with diarrhoea — early dehydration signs requiring prompt oral rehydration.
A cluster of cases with similar symptoms in the same household or community after a shared water source, indicating a waterborne outbreak.
emergencySigns: Profuse watery diarrhoea (more than 3 litres per hour in adults) with vomiting and inability to maintain oral fluid intake, indicating severe cholera dehydration — a medical emergency requiring immediate IV fluid resuscitation.
Signs of shock in a child with acute diarrhoea: sunken fontanelle, absent tears, cold extremities, altered consciousness, capillary refill over 3 seconds.
Intestinal perforation in typhoid fever: sudden-onset severe abdominal pain, board-like rigidity, and clinical deterioration after several days of fever.
Hepatic encephalopathy in hepatitis E during pregnancy: confusion, jaundice, and coagulopathy (abnormal bleeding) require urgent obstetric and hepatology input.
Haemolytic uraemic syndrome (HUS) in E. coli O157 infection: sudden reduction in urine output, pallor, and confusion following bloody diarrhoea in a child.

causes.cause: Faecal contamination of drinking water sources
causes.description: Open defecation near water sources, broken sewage infrastructure, flooding that overflows sewage systems into water supplies, and poorly sealed latrines allow intestinal pathogens (Vibrio cholerae, Salmonella typhi, E. coli, hepatitis A virus) to enter wells, rivers, and municipal distribution systems at concentrations sufficient to cause infection.

causes.cause: Inadequate water treatment and distribution system failures
causes.description: Failure of chlorination at water treatment plants, intermittent water supply allowing back-siphonage of contaminated water into pipes, and absence of end-point water treatment in homes allow viable pathogens to reach consumers. Cryptosporidium and Giardia oocysts/cysts resist standard chlorination and require filtration or UV treatment.

causes.cause: Poor hand hygiene and sanitation behaviours
causes.description: Hand washing with soap before food preparation and after toilet use breaks the faeco-oral transmission chain; in communities where soap and water are unavailable or cultural practices do not include routine hand washing, contact transmission of E. coli, hepatitis A, and shigellosis is substantially amplified.

causes.cause: Contact with contaminated recreational or occupational water
causes.description: Wading or swimming in slow-moving freshwater bodies harbouring Schistosoma cercariae — particularly in sub-Saharan Africa, the Nile basin, and parts of South America and Asia — causes schistosomiasis. Occupational water contact (rice farming, irrigation) accounts for the majority of the 240 million cases globally.

causes.cause: Naturally occurring chemical contamination of groundwater
causes.description: In many regions of Bangladesh, India, Pakistan, and parts of Africa and Latin America, groundwater contains naturally elevated arsenic (above the WHO guideline of 10 μg/L) or fluoride (above 1.5 mg/L), causing arsenicosis or fluorosis in populations that depend on deep tubewells for drinking water without testing or treatment.

causes.cause: Contamination of irrigation water used on food crops
causes.description: Wastewater and contaminated river water used for irrigating vegetables can deposit bacterial and viral pathogens and heavy metals on produce; cholera, typhoid, and hepatitis A have been traced to salads and raw vegetables irrigated with contaminated water in multiple outbreak investigations.

Displacement, flooding, cyclones, and conflict destroy water and sanitation infrastructure simultaneously while concentrating large populations in camps with inadequate latrines and no running water; cholera, typhoid, and hepatitis E epidemics are predictable consequences within days to weeks of such events.

riskFactors.factor: Living in a low- or middle-income country without piped treated water
riskFactors.description: Approximately 2 billion people worldwide lack access to safe managed drinking water; the absence of effective water treatment, distribution infrastructure, and sanitation coverage is the primary structural risk factor driving waterborne disease burden.

Children under five have immature immune systems with limited prior exposure to waterborne pathogens, lower buffering capacity against dehydration, and higher contact with contaminated surfaces; they account for approximately 70% of diarrhoeal disease deaths globally.

riskFactors.factor: Open defecation practices in the community
riskFactors.description: Communities where 20-30% or more of members practise open defecation have significantly higher rates of child stunting, enteric infection, and diarrhoeal disease even for households using improved sanitation, because environmental faecal contamination of water, soil, and food is pervasive.

HIV/AIDS, chemotherapy, malnutrition, and high-dose corticosteroids dramatically extend the duration and severity of Cryptosporidium, Giardia, and non-typhoidal Salmonella infections; Cryptosporidium can cause cholera-like life-threatening diarrhoea in patients with advanced HIV.

riskFactors.factor: Travel to endemic areas without precautions
riskFactors.description: Travellers from high-income countries to areas with inadequate sanitation account for a significant proportion of hepatitis A, typhoid, and traveller's diarrhoea cases presenting in clinical settings; pre-travel vaccination, water treatment, and food hygiene reduce this risk substantially.

Flooding contaminates household water supplies and creates standing water supporting leptospiral survival and mosquito breeding; cholera and leptospirosis outbreaks follow within 1-4 weeks of major floods in endemic regions.

riskFactors.factor: Agricultural water contact in endemic areas
riskFactors.description: Farmers and agricultural workers who wade in irrigation canals in schistosomiasis-endemic areas (sub-Saharan Africa, Egypt, Brazil, China, Philippines) face the highest occupational exposure risk; rubber boots and cercaricidal creams reduce but do not eliminate risk.

riskFactors.factor: Reliance on unprotected wells or surface water
riskFactors.description: Households using open wells, rivers, or unprotected springs as primary water sources have 2-5 times higher rates of diarrhoeal disease than those using piped or treated water; simple household water treatment (chlorination, boiling, solar disinfection) reduces this risk by 30-50%.

affectedDemographics: Children under 5 in sub-Saharan Africa and South Asia, who bear the majority of diarrhoeal disease deaths
Communities in low- and middle-income countries without piped treated water or improved sanitation
International travellers to endemic regions, in whom typhoid, hepatitis A, and traveller's diarrhoea cluster
Agricultural and fishing communities with regular freshwater contact in schistosomiasis-endemic countries
People in humanitarian emergency and displacement settings
Populations in Bangladesh, India, and parts of Africa dependent on arsenic- or fluoride-rich groundwater
Immunocompromised individuals, including those with HIV/AIDS, who experience disproportionately severe and prolonged waterborne infections
diagnosisOverview: Diagnosis of waterborne diseases relies on the clinical presentation, exposure history, geographic context, and targeted microbiological or parasitological testing. In endemic settings or during outbreak investigation, the combination of acute watery diarrhoea and a plausible exposure to contaminated water has high predictive value. For individual case diagnosis: stool culture (Salmonella, Shigella, Campylobacter, Vibrio) and stool microscopy or immunoassay (Giardia, Cryptosporidium, Entamoeba) are the first-line investigations for diarrhoeal illness. Rapid antigen tests for cholera, typhoid (Widal test, though limited specificity; blood culture is the gold standard), and hepatitis A/E IgM serology provide expedited diagnosis when specific pathogens are suspected. Stool PCR multiplex panels, where available, detect multiple pathogens simultaneously with high sensitivity and are particularly useful in returned travellers and immunocompromised patients. For schistosomiasis, stool or urine microscopy for eggs is the traditional method, but serology (ELISA) and antigen detection in urine are increasingly used. Blood tests including full blood count (eosinophilia in helminthic infections), liver function tests (hepatitis), and renal function (haemolytic uraemic syndrome in E. coli O157) guide severity assessment. In chronic arsenicosis and fluorosis, clinical signs and drinking water arsenic or fluoride measurement confirm the diagnosis. Environmental water testing — E. coli counts, turbidity, chlorine residual — is essential for outbreak investigation and for directing public health interventions at the population level.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Identifies bacterial pathogens — Vibrio cholerae, Salmonella typhi/paratyphi, Shigella, Campylobacter, pathogenic E. coli — and provides antibiotic sensitivities, essential given widespread fluoroquinolone resistance in typhoid.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: A stool sample is collected in a sterile container and sent to the microbiology laboratory; results take 48-72 hours. Multiple samples increase yield for typhoid, where blood culture is often more sensitive than stool early in illness.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Detects cysts and trophozoites of Giardia lamblia, Cryptosporidium parvum, and Entamoeba histolytica; antigen EIA tests are more sensitive than microscopy for Giardia and Cryptosporidium.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Three stool samples taken on alternate days maximise sensitivity because cyst shedding is intermittent; the fresh sample is examined under a microscope or processed for antigen testing in the laboratory.

diagnosticTests.test: Blood culture (for typhoid and paratyphoid fever)
diagnosticTests.purpose: The gold standard for diagnosing Salmonella typhi and paratyphi bacteraemia in the first week of fever; sensitivity peaks at 80-90% in the first 7-10 days before antibiotics.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: 20 mL of blood is drawn from a vein (ideally during a fever spike) and inoculated into aerobic and anaerobic culture bottles; results take 3-5 days but alert systems notify the laboratory of positive results early.

diagnosticTests.test: Hepatitis A and E serology (IgM antibodies)
diagnosticTests.purpose: Confirms acute hepatitis A (anti-HAV IgM) or hepatitis E (anti-HEV IgM) in patients presenting with acute jaundice after a water or food exposure, distinguishing viral hepatitis from other causes.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: A blood draw from the arm; IgM serology is usually positive from the time of jaundice onset; results take 24-48 hours at standard laboratories.

diagnosticTests.test: Urine or stool microscopy for Schistosoma eggs
diagnosticTests.purpose: Detects Schistosoma haematobium eggs in urine (peak at noon collection) or S. mansoni eggs in stool, confirming diagnosis; egg counts indicate infection intensity.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: A midday urine sample or three stool samples on alternate days; the sample is filtered and examined microscopically. Sensitivity is higher with multiple samples and in heavier infections.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Simultaneously detects bacterial, viral, and parasitic pathogens in stool within 4-8 hours, providing comprehensive diagnosis in returned travellers, immunocompromised patients, or severe unexplained diarrhoea.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: A rectal swab or stool sample; results from panels such as BioFire FilmArray or Luminex xTAG return within a few hours. Positive results may need interpretation in the clinical context as the panel detects DNA rather than confirming active disease.

diagnosticTests.test: Drinking water testing (E. coli and chemical parameters)
diagnosticTests.purpose: Confirms faecal contamination of a water source in outbreak investigation or for household water quality assessment; arsenic and fluoride measurement guides chemical contamination management.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: A water sample is collected in a sterile bottle from the suspected source; E. coli culture takes 24 hours; chemical analysis by spectrophotometry takes 24-72 hours at a public health laboratory.

treatmentOverview: Treatment of water and sanitation-related diseases is guided by the specific pathogen, severity, and setting. The single most universally applicable intervention is oral rehydration therapy (ORT) — a WHO/UNICEF formulation of glucose, sodium, potassium, and citrate dissolved in clean water. ORT reduces mortality from cholera and other acute watery diarrhoeas by more than 95% and is the cornerstone of management at all levels of care. Intravenous fluid resuscitation (Ringer's lactate at 100 mL/kg over 3-5 hours for severe cholera) is reserved for patients too obtunded or vomiting to take ORT. Antibiotics reduce duration of cholera by about 50% and are recommended for moderate-to-severe cases: doxycycline single dose, azithromycin, or ciprofloxacin are first-line depending on local resistance. Typhoid fever is treated with azithromycin (now preferred first-line because of widespread fluoroquinolone resistance) or, for severe cases, intravenous cephalosporins (ceftriaxone). The emergence of extensively drug-resistant (XDR) typhoid in Pakistan has prompted use of azithromycin and carbapenems in the most resistant strains. Hepatitis A and uncomplicated hepatitis E are self-limiting and treated supportively; pregnant women with hepatitis E require intensive supportive care and early delivery consideration. Giardiasis is treated with metronidazole (5-7 days), tinidazole (single dose), or nitazoxanide; cryptosporidiosis in immunocompetent patients is usually self-limiting but severe disease in immunocompromised patients is treated with nitazoxanide and immune reconstitution. Amoebiasis is treated with metronidazole followed by a luminal agent (paromomycin or diloxanide) to eradicate cysts. Schistosomiasis is treated with a single oral dose of praziquantel (40 mg/kg), which is over 85% effective. Zinc supplementation (10 mg/day in children under 5, 20 mg/day in older children) reduces diarrhoeal episode duration and prevents recurrence and is recommended by WHO alongside ORT for all childhood diarrhoea. At the public health level, mass drug administration with praziquantel, pre-exposure vaccination (typhoid conjugate vaccine, hepatitis A vaccine), and water and sanitation infrastructure investment are the interventions that reduce disease burden at scale.

medicalTreatments.name: Oral rehydration therapy (ORT)
medicalTreatments.description: WHO/UNICEF low-osmolarity oral rehydration solution (ORS) containing 75 mmol/L sodium, 75 mmol/L glucose, 20 mmol/L potassium, and 10 mmol/L citrate corrects the dehydration and electrolyte losses of acute diarrhoeal disease without intravenous access. Administered in small frequent sips; a child should receive 50-100 mL/kg over 4 hours for moderate dehydration, then replacement of ongoing losses.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Reduces cholera case fatality from 25-50% to below 1% when administered correctly; WHO estimates ORT saves approximately 60 million lives annually.

medicalTreatments.name: Doxycycline (or azithromycin) for cholera
medicalTreatments.description: A single oral dose of doxycycline 300 mg (or azithromycin 1 g) in adults dramatically reduces the volume and duration of watery stool in cholera, from roughly 5 days to 2-3 days. Azithromycin is preferred in pregnant women and in areas with doxycycline-resistant V. cholerae. Both should be preceded by ORT to correct dehydration.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Reduces stool volume and duration of illness by approximately 50% in cholera; bacteriological cure above 90% in susceptible strains.

medicalTreatments.name: Azithromycin for typhoid fever (uncomplicated)
medicalTreatments.description: Azithromycin 500-1000 mg orally for 5-7 days is now the preferred first-line treatment for uncomplicated typhoid in WHO guidelines because of widespread fluoroquinolone resistance, particularly in the Indian subcontinent and parts of Africa. It is also safe in children and pregnancy.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Clinical cure in approximately 85-95% of susceptible cases; defervescence in 3-5 days. Multidrug-resistant typhoid responds to azithromycin or carbapenems.

medicalTreatments.name: Praziquantel for schistosomiasis
medicalTreatments.description: A single oral dose of praziquantel 40 mg/kg kills adult Schistosoma worms by disrupting calcium homeostasis in the parasite, causing irreversible muscle contraction and tegumental damage. It is the mainstay of both individual treatment and WHO-led mass drug administration programmes.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Parasitological cure in 60-90% of treated cases after a single dose; a second dose at 4 weeks raises cure rates to over 85% in high-intensity infections.

medicalTreatments.name: Metronidazole for giardiasis and amoebiasis
medicalTreatments.description: Metronidazole 400-500 mg three times daily for 5-7 days is first-line for both Giardia lamblia intestinalis and invasive Entamoeba histolytica; tinidazole as a single 2 g dose is an equally effective and better-tolerated alternative for giardiasis. A luminal agent (paromomycin) must follow to eradicate intraluminal cysts after invasive amoebiasis treatment.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Symptomatic and parasitological cure in 85-95% of Giardia cases; approximately 90% efficacy for intestinal amoebiasis; amebic liver abscess requires extended treatment.

medicalTreatments.name: Zinc supplementation (adjunct to ORT in children)
medicalTreatments.description: WHO and UNICEF recommend zinc 10 mg/day for children under 6 months and 20 mg/day for children 6 months to 5 years for 10-14 days alongside ORS for all acute diarrhoeal episodes. Zinc reduces episode duration, severity, and the risk of new episodes in the following 2-3 months.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Reduces duration of acute diarrhoeal episodes by approximately 25% and risk of persistent diarrhoea by about 40% in systematic reviews of randomised trials.

surgicalOptions.name: Surgical management of typhoid intestinal perforation
surgicalOptions.description: Perforation of the distal ileum occurs in 1-3% of typhoid cases; requires emergency laparotomy with primary repair or resection and anastomosis, combined with broad-spectrum antibiotic cover. Delay dramatically increases mortality.
surgicalOptions.successRate: Mortality of 10-32% even with surgery in resource-limited settings; early surgical intervention with adequate post-operative antibiotic therapy achieves survival in 70-90% in well-equipped settings.

surgicalOptions.name: Drainage of hepatic or splenic abscess (invasive amoebiasis)
surgicalOptions.description: Amoebic liver abscesses that do not respond to metronidazole within 72 hours, are large (over 5 cm), are at risk of rupture into the pericardium or pleura, or involve the left lobe of the liver require ultrasound-guided percutaneous aspiration or surgical drainage.
surgicalOptions.successRate: Percutaneous drainage combined with metronidazole achieves resolution in 85-95% of cases; open surgical drainage is reserved for inaccessible or ruptured abscesses.

alternativeTreatments.name: Household water treatment and storage (HWTS)
alternativeTreatments.description: Point-of-use water treatment methods — boiling, solar disinfection (SODIS), ceramic pot filtration, and household chlorination — applied without piped treated water supply reduce household diarrhoeal disease risk by 30-50% in randomised controlled trials. WHO promotes these as interim measures while infrastructure is developed.

alternativeTreatments.name: Zinc-enhanced rice-based ORS
alternativeTreatments.description: Rice-based oral rehydration solutions with higher glucose polymer content produce less osmotic stool and faster absorption than standard glucose-based ORS in cholera; locally available and culturally acceptable in South and Southeast Asia, particularly for home management of cholera in endemic areas.

alternativeTreatments.name: Probiotics as adjunct therapy
alternativeTreatments.description: Lactobacillus rhamnosus GG and Saccharomyces boulardii have Cochrane-review-level evidence for reducing the duration of acute infectious diarrhoea by approximately one day in children; they are adjuncts to ORS and standard treatment, not replacements, and are not indicated for cholera or typhoid.

whySeeSpecialist: Uncomplicated watery diarrhoea can be managed with ORT in primary care or at home. A specialist in infectious disease or tropical medicine should evaluate patients with: suspected typhoid fever (sustained high fever requiring blood culture), jaundice suggesting hepatitis A or E, suspected schistosomiasis or other helminthic infection, failure of first-line antibiotic therapy, immunocompromised status complicating any waterborne infection, or any sign of severe dehydration, peritonitis, or systemic sepsis. Public health authorities should be notified of suspected cholera, typhoid, or hepatitis A to trigger outbreak investigation.
doctorSelectionGuide: For acute diarrhoeal illness in a returned traveller or in an individual with exposure to an unimproved water source, an infectious disease physician or travel medicine specialist provides the most targeted evaluation, including PCR stool panels, serology, and antibiotic resistance-guided therapy. For schistosomiasis or other tropical parasitic infections, a tropical medicine specialist or parasitologist is appropriate. Preventive and public health physicians are the correct specialty for outbreak investigation, water quality assessment, and community-level intervention. In endemic settings, community health workers and primary care clinics are the first-responders for ORT and basic antibiotic management.
preventionStrategies: Ensure access to water from a safely managed source — piped treated water, protected borehole, or rainwater harvested with storage; if uncertain, boil, filter, or disinfect water before drinking.

Wash hands with soap and water for at least 20 seconds before food preparation, before eating, and after using the toilet; this single behaviour reduces diarrhoeal disease risk by approximately 40-48%.

Vaccinate against typhoid (typhoid conjugate vaccine, one dose, effective for at least 4 years) and hepatitis A (two doses, lifelong protection); recommended for all children in endemic areas and for travellers.
Use sanitation facilities and avoid open defecation; improved latrines break the faeco-oral chain at the community level, with health gains that extend beyond the household.
Avoid raw or undercooked food irrigated with wastewater, shellfish from contaminated waters, and street food where hygiene cannot be verified during travel.
Avoid freshwater contact in schistosomiasis-endemic areas; dry vigorously with a towel immediately after accidental contact, as cercariae may not have penetrated fully in the first few minutes.
Treat drinking water sources and test for arsenic and fluoride in areas where these contaminants are endemic; use alternative deep or rainwater sources, or install appropriate treatment filters.

Participate in mass drug administration (MDA) programmes with praziquantel for schistosomiasis or albendazole/mebendazole for soil-transmitted helminths where these are offered by national programmes.
lifestyleModifications: Begin ORS at the earliest sign of diarrhoea without waiting for a clinician's instruction; a child's first sign of loose stool should trigger immediate preparation of ORS.
Store drinking water in a clean covered container and do not dip hands or cups directly into it.
Keep latrines clean and at least 10 metres from water sources and food preparation areas.
Wash all raw vegetables and fruit with safe water; peel root vegetables where water quality is uncertain.
Boil water for infant formula preparation regardless of apparent tap water safety in settings with inadequate monitoring.
Report suspected cases of cholera or typhoid to the local health authority immediately, because cluster investigation prevents community spread.

recommended: Continued breastfeeding for infants during diarrhoeal illness, which maintains immunoglobulin delivery and caloric intake
ORS frequently in small sips; avoid the mistaken practice of withholding fluids
Zinc-rich foods (meat, legumes, nuts, dairy) or zinc supplementation during and after diarrhoeal episodes in children
Gradual reintroduction of normal diet as soon as tolerated — starving during diarrhoea prolongs gut recovery
Boiled or thoroughly cooked starchy foods (rice, potato, banana) in the early recovery phase if solid food is tolerated
avoid: Sugary drinks, undiluted fruit juices, and carbonated beverages, which worsen osmotic diarrhoea
Raw or incompletely cooked shellfish (oysters, clams), which concentrate hepatitis A virus, Vibrio, and norovirus from contaminated water
Tap or stream water without treatment in areas with inadequate WASH infrastructure
Dairy products during the acute phase of giardiasis and cryptosporidiosis, which may provoke lactase deficiency-related bloating and loose stool

exerciseGuidelines: Rest during the acute febrile or dehydration phase of any waterborne illness; physical activity increases metabolic water demand and electrolyte loss and worsens dehydration. Typhoid fever specifically requires strict rest because physical exertion during the second and third weeks of illness is a recognised precipitant of intestinal perforation. Return to light activity can begin once fever has been absent for at least 48 hours, no dehydration is present, and appetite is recovering. Full physical activity is resumed when clinically well, which may take 1-4 weeks for enteric fever and longer for hepatitis A-related fatigue.
dailyManagement: Monitor fluid balance closely during active diarrhoeal illness: weigh the child at every clinic visit, track stool output, and escalate from ORS to IV fluids if >10% dehydration is estimated.
Complete the full prescribed antibiotic course for typhoid and cholera, even once fever settles; incomplete courses drive resistance and relapse.
Notify household members and local health authorities of typhoid, cholera, or hepatitis A diagnoses to trigger contact tracing and prophylaxis.
For chronic schistosomiasis follow-up, repeat stool or urine microscopy 4-6 weeks after praziquantel to confirm egg clearance and re-treat if needed.
Maintain ongoing water quality testing and household treatment if living in an area with arsenic or fluoride contamination until a safe alternative water source is established.
Support household members in hand washing behaviour change; education about the faeco-oral route and supervision of young children's hand washing behaviour is as important as any drug treatment.
prognosis: Prognosis varies substantially across the spectrum of waterborne diseases. Acute watery diarrhoea including cholera carries near-zero mortality with timely ORT; untreated severe cholera carries 25-50% fatality. Typhoid fever has a case fatality rate of 1-4% with adequate antibiotic therapy and 10-30% without; intestinal perforation raises mortality to 10-30% even with surgery. Hepatitis A resolves fully in the great majority; fulminant hepatic failure occurs in approximately 0.1-0.3% of cases, predominantly in older adults and those with pre-existing liver disease. Hepatitis E in pregnancy carries up to 25% mortality from fulminant liver failure. Giardiasis and cryptosporidiosis in immunocompetent patients resolve with treatment; in advanced HIV, cryptosporidiosis can be life-threatening. Chronic schistosomiasis causing periportal fibrosis and portal hypertension (hepatosplenic schistosomiasis) has significantly reduced quality of life and survival without treatment; annual praziquantel under WHO MDA programmes markedly reduces morbidity. Arsenicosis and fluorosis are progressive with continued exposure but stabilise and partially reverse with provision of safe water. Globally, access to safe water and sanitation is estimated by WHO to prevent 9.1% of the total global burden of disease — among the highest returns of any public health investment.
recoveryTimeline: Uncomplicated watery diarrhoea from ETEC or rotavirus resolves in 2-5 days with ORT. Cholera symptoms resolve in 3-5 days with ORT alone; antibiotics shorten this to 2-3 days. Typhoid fever defervescence occurs within 3-7 days of effective antibiotics; full recovery takes 2-4 weeks. Hepatitis A: jaundice peaks at week 2 and resolves over 4-6 weeks; fatigue may persist 2-3 months. Giardiasis responds to metronidazole within 1-2 weeks; persistent symptoms suggest reinfection or co-existing lactase deficiency. Schistosomiasis: egg counts fall to zero 6-12 weeks after praziquantel; established fibrosis does not reverse but inflammation resolves. Arsenicosis keratosis partially reverses over months to years after safe water provision.
complications: Severe dehydration and hypovolaemic shock — the leading cause of cholera death and a serious risk in any acute high-volume diarrhoeal illness in young children; preventable with early ORS.
Intestinal perforation in typhoid fever — occurs in 1-3% of untreated or inadequately treated cases during the third or fourth week of illness; associated with 10-30% mortality even with surgery.

Post-infectious irritable bowel syndrome (PI-IBS) — develops in approximately 10-15% of individuals after enteric infection (Giardia, Campylobacter, Salmonella) and produces chronic abdominal discomfort and altered bowel habit for months to years.

Haemolytic uraemic syndrome (HUS) — complicates 5-10% of E. coli O157 infections in children; the triad of microangiopathic haemolytic anaemia, thrombocytopenia, and acute kidney injury carries 2-5% mortality and 25% risk of long-term renal impairment.
Periportal fibrosis and portal hypertension — chronic hepatosplenic schistosomiasis produces oesophageal varices and life-threatening haematemesis in heavily infected individuals in endemic areas.

Fluorosis-related skeletal deformity — chronic high fluoride exposure produces crippling skeletal fluorosis with joint pain, limited mobility, and neurological compression from bone overgrowth affecting tens of millions globally.

supportResources.description: CDC surveillance data and guidance on waterborne disease prevention, treatment, and investigation.

supportResources.name: UNICEF — Diarrhoea: Treatment and Prevention
supportResources.description: UNICEF resources on oral rehydration therapy, zinc supplementation, and childhood diarrhoea prevention.

confusedWithConditions.name: Amoebiasis
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Amoebiasis caused by Entamoeba histolytica specifically produces bloody dysentery or liver abscess through tissue invasion and requires metronidazole plus a luminal agent; generic watery traveller's diarrhoea is usually caused by non-invasive E. coli or viral pathogens and is self-limiting with ORT.

confusedWithConditions.name: Leptospirosis
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Leptospirosis is acquired from freshwater or soil contaminated with animal urine, causing fever, myalgia, and jaundice that can resemble typhoid or hepatitis A, but is diagnosed by serological testing (MAT) and treated with penicillin or doxycycline rather than enteric fever antibiotics.

confusedWithConditions.name: Typhoid fever
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Typhoid fever (Salmonella typhi) specifically causes a sustained fever over 7-14 days with relative bradycardia and rose spots, distinguished from non-specific watery diarrhoea by its systemic nature and confirmed by blood culture; it requires specific antibiotic therapy to prevent life-threatening complications.

confusedWithConditions.name: Schistosomiasis
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Schistosomiasis is acquired by freshwater skin contact, not oral ingestion, and causes haematuria or hepatosplenic complications in the chronic phase after weeks to months of latency, contrasting with the acute watery diarrhoea of most orally transmitted waterborne infections.

relatedConditions.name: Cholera
relatedConditions.relevance: Cholera is the most rapidly fatal waterborne disease, requiring same-day oral or intravenous rehydration to prevent death; it is the epidemic archetype of WASH failure at the community level.

relatedConditions.name: Typhoid fever
relatedConditions.relevance: Typhoid is a systemic waterborne bacterial infection requiring specific antibiotics; its resurgence as extensively drug-resistant typhoid in Pakistan and parts of Africa highlights the consequences of inadequate water and sanitation.

relatedConditions.name: Hepatitis A
relatedConditions.relevance: Hepatitis A virus is transmitted by faecally contaminated water and food; it is vaccine-preventable, making it a model for the interaction between WASH and immunisation strategies in reducing waterborne disease.

relatedConditions.name: Schistosomiasis
relatedConditions.relevance: Schistosomiasis affects 240 million people through freshwater contact and is a leading cause of chronic morbidity in sub-Saharan Africa; its control requires water contact reduction combined with praziquantel MDA.

relatedConditions.name: E. coli infection
relatedConditions.relevance: Enterotoxigenic and enterohaemorrhagic E. coli are leading waterborne causes of traveller's diarrhoea and HUS respectively, transmitted via contaminated water and food globally.

relatedConditions.name: Leptospirosis
relatedConditions.relevance: Leptospirosis overlaps with water-sanitation disease through its transmission via flood water and animal-contaminated water sources, particularly in tropical regions during the rainy season.

faqs.question: What is a water and sanitation disease?
faqs.answer: Water and sanitation diseases are illnesses caused by consuming or contacting water contaminated with pathogens or chemicals, or from inadequate hygiene. They include cholera, typhoid, hepatitis A, giardia, schistosomiasis, and chronic chemical syndromes like arsenicosis. They cause 1.4 million deaths annually worldwide.

faqs.question: How do you get cholera?
faqs.answer: Cholera is contracted by drinking water or eating food contaminated with Vibrio cholerae bacteria from human faecal matter. It does not spread directly person-to-person in most cases. Outbreaks follow breakdowns in sanitation — floods, conflicts, or displaced population settings without clean water access.

faqs.question: What is oral rehydration therapy and how does it work?
faqs.answer: Oral rehydration therapy (ORS) is a solution of water, salt, and sugar formulated by WHO that replaces fluid and electrolytes lost in diarrhoea. The glucose in ORS activates a sodium-glucose co-transporter in the gut lining that continues to absorb fluid even during active secretory diarrhoea, reducing dehydration without IV access.

faqs.question: Is typhoid fever dangerous?
faqs.answer: Yes. Untreated typhoid carries a 10-30% fatality rate and can cause intestinal perforation, haemorrhage, or sepsis. With appropriate antibiotic therapy, case fatality falls to 1-4%. Drug-resistant typhoid is an increasing concern; blood culture-guided antibiotic selection is important.

faqs.question: Can you get hepatitis from drinking contaminated water?
faqs.answer: Yes. Hepatitis A and hepatitis E viruses are transmitted via faecally contaminated water and food. Hepatitis A is vaccine-preventable and usually self-limiting; hepatitis E is especially dangerous in pregnant women with a fatality rate up to 25%.

faqs.question: How is giardia infection treated?
faqs.answer: Giardiasis is treated with a 5-7 day course of metronidazole or a single dose of tinidazole, both of which achieve parasitological cure in 85-95% of cases. Nitazoxanide is an alternative. Reinfection is common if the contaminated water source is not addressed.

faqs.question: What is schistosomiasis?
faqs.answer: Schistosomiasis (bilharzia) is a parasitic infection caused by Schistosoma worms acquired by wading or swimming in freshwater containing cercariae. It affects 240 million people globally, mainly in sub-Saharan Africa. It causes haematuria in urogenital form and liver disease in intestinal form. A single dose of praziquantel treats it.

faqs.question: How do you prevent waterborne diseases when travelling?
faqs.answer: Drink only bottled, boiled, or UV-treated water; avoid ice made from tap water; eat thoroughly cooked food; peel fruit yourself; wash hands with soap regularly; vaccinate against typhoid and hepatitis A before travel to endemic areas; and avoid swimming in freshwater in schistosomiasis-endemic regions.

faqs.question: Is diarrhoea in children an emergency?
faqs.answer: Diarrhoea with signs of dehydration — sunken eyes, no tears, dry mouth, reduced urine, or lethargy — in a child is an emergency requiring immediate ORS and medical assessment. Profuse watery diarrhoea with vomiting requires same-day care. A child who cannot drink or is unconscious needs IV fluids urgently.

faqs.question: What is the most effective way to prevent waterborne disease?
faqs.answer: Access to safely managed piped treated water is the single most effective intervention. Where infrastructure is absent, hand washing with soap reduces diarrhoeal risk by 40-48%. Typhoid conjugate vaccine and hepatitis A vaccine provide specific protection. Household water treatment (boiling, chlorination) is an effective interim measure.

faqs.question: How does arsenic in water affect health?
faqs.answer: Long-term drinking of water containing arsenic above 10 μg/L causes arsenicosis: hardened skin patches (keratosis), skin colour changes, and increased risk of skin, lung, bladder, and kidney cancers. Peripheral neuropathy and cardiovascular disease also occur. Switching to a safe water source stabilises and partially reverses early changes.

faqs.question: What is the treatment for schistosomiasis?
faqs.answer: Schistosomiasis is treated with a single oral dose of praziquantel at 40 mg/kg, which kills adult worms. It achieves parasitological cure in 60-90% of cases; a second dose after 4 weeks is given for heavy infections. Mass drug administration with praziquantel is the WHO strategy to control the disease in endemic communities.

faqs.question: Can fluoride in drinking water be harmful?
faqs.answer: Fluoride below 1.5 mg/L is beneficial for dental health. Above this level, dental fluorosis causes mottled or pitted teeth. At very high levels (above 4 mg/L in chronic exposure), skeletal fluorosis causes joint pain, bone deformity, and neurological complications. Testing local water and using alternative sources or defluoridation filters prevents harm.

faqs.question: Why do children die from diarrhoea?
faqs.answer: Diarrhoea kills primarily through dehydration. Children under 5 have less body fluid reserve, lose proportionally more fluid per kilogram with each episode, and progress to shock faster than adults. Nearly all diarrhoeal deaths are preventable with timely ORS, zinc supplementation, and access to basic healthcare.

faqs.question: What causes typhoid fever?
faqs.answer: Typhoid fever is caused by Salmonella typhi, a bacterium transmitted through water or food contaminated with the faeces or urine of an infected person or a chronic carrier. One to 2 teaspoons of contaminated water can transmit the infection. The bacterium invades the gut wall and enters the bloodstream, causing sustained high fever.

faqs.question: How long does typhoid fever last?
faqs.answer: Without treatment, typhoid fever lasts 3-4 weeks with progressive fever, confusion, and risk of intestinal perforation. With appropriate antibiotic therapy (azithromycin or ceftriaxone), fever resolves within 3-7 days and full recovery takes 2-4 weeks. Chronic carriers (1-5% of cases) can transmit infection for months or years after recovery.

faqs.question: Is there a vaccine for waterborne diseases?
faqs.answer: Effective vaccines exist for typhoid (typhoid conjugate vaccine, one dose, 4+ years of protection) and hepatitis A (two-dose series, lifelong protection). No licensed vaccines exist for cholera for individual protection in most clinical settings, though oral cholera vaccine is used in outbreak control. Rotavirus vaccine prevents a major cause of childhood diarrhoea.

faqs.question: What is the swimmer's itch from schistosomiasis?
faqs.answer: Swimmer's itch is an itchy papular rash that develops within hours of cercarial penetration of the skin when entering freshwater harbouring Schistosoma. It is the early skin phase of schistosomiasis infection. Vigorously towelling dry within seconds of leaving the water may remove some cercariae before they penetrate fully.

metaTitle: Water and Sanitation Disease: Symptoms & Treatment | aihealz
metaDescription: Cholera, typhoid and giardia spread through contaminated water. Learn symptoms, oral rehydration treatment and prevention strategies. Doctor-reviewed.
